title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read an article from which six sentences have been removed. Choose from the sentences A–G the one which fits each gap (37–42). There is one extra sentence which you do not need to use.

content.title: Writing on Walls: Street Art and Cultural Identity
content.text: In the early hours of the morning, while most of the city sleeps, a figure in dark clothing works quickly with a spray can on a blank concrete wall. By dawn, a vibrant mural depicting local heroes, political messages or surreal landscapes has transformed a dull urban surface into something worth stopping for. (37) [37] Este proceso, repetido en miles de ciudades, define lo que hoy conocemos como arte urbano. [/37] Street art has evolved from an act of rebellion into a globally recognised art form that commands serious critical and commercial attention. || Its roots lie in the graffiti subculture that emerged in New York City in the late 1960s and 1970s. Young people from marginalised communities used public walls as a canvas to assert their identity and claim visibility in a society that largely ignored them. (38) [38] El grafiti fue desde el principio una forma de dar voz a quienes no tenían otro medio de expresión. [/38] The style spread rapidly to other cities, mutating as it went, absorbing influences from hip-hop, skateboarding and graphic design. || The line between vandalism and art has always been contested. When the British artist Banksy began stencilling wry, politically charged images on walls across Bristol and London in the 1990s, he was technically committing criminal damage. (39) [39] Sin embargo, sus obras empezaron a atraer multitudes y a ser consideradas patrimonio cultural. [/39] Today, councils that once painted over his work now preserve it behind protective screens and celebrate it as a tourist attraction. || Many cities have responded to street art with more nuanced policies. Rather than pursuing blanket criminalisation, urban authorities in cities like Melbourne, Berlin and São Paulo have designated 'free walls' where artists can work legally. (40) [40] Estas zonas autorizadas han permitido el florecimiento de escenas artísticas locales vibrantes. [/40] The result has been the emergence of open-air galleries that attract visitors from around the world and provide young artists with a professional platform. || The commercialisation of street art has sparked debate within the community. Galleries, auction houses and advertising agencies have all sought to appropriate the aesthetic for profit. (41) [41] Algunos artistas lo ven como una traición a los valores originales del movimiento. [/41] Others argue that commercial success simply extends an artist's reach and provides the resources needed to continue working at scale. || Whatever one's view, street art has permanently altered the visual landscape of the modern city. (42) [42] Hoy en día, los murales son parte integrante de la identidad de muchos barrios urbanos. [/42] A neighbourhood mural can become a landmark, a source of collective pride and a catalyst for wider regeneration — all from a few cans of spray paint and a willingness to be seen.

paragraphs.A: From the outset, graffiti served as a form of self-expression for communities with few other platforms.
paragraphs.B: These legal zones have allowed vibrant local art scenes to develop openly and professionally.
paragraphs.C: Yet his images quickly attracted crowds and came to be treated as cultural landmarks worth protecting.
paragraphs.D: Today, murals have become defining features of many urban neighbourhoods, shaping local identity.
paragraphs.E: The international art market has assigned multi-million-pound price tags to works originally created illegally on public walls.
paragraphs.F: For some artists, being absorbed into the commercial mainstream feels like a betrayal of the movement's origins.
paragraphs.G: This process, repeated across thousands of cities worldwide, has given rise to what we now call street art.

questions.explanation: G generaliza la escena descrita al inicio del artículo y la enmarca dentro del concepto de arte urbano global.
A explica el papel del grafiti como medio de expresión para comunidades marginadas, conectando con la difusión posterior del estilo.
C introduce el contraste entre el acto ilegal de Banksy y la posterior admiración pública, usando 'sin embargo' implícito.
B describe el resultado positivo de las zonas legales para el arte, enlazando con la creación de galerías al aire libre.
F expresa la postura crítica de los artistas ante la comercialización, que el siguiente párrafo contrasta con la visión favorable.
D cierra el artículo afirmando el papel del arte urbano en la identidad de los barrios, reforzando la conclusión positiva.
